label: The Alamo, San Antonio
sub: Mission-fort where ~189 Texan defenders were killed by Santa Anna's army on March 6, 1836. Site of the most iconic battle of the Texas Revolution.

label: Battle of Gonzales
sub: Opening engagement of the Texas Revolution on October 2, 1835. Texans refused to surrender a cannon and repelled Mexican soldiers under the 'Come and Take It' flag.

label: Goliad — La Bahía / Massacre Site
sub: Presidio La Bahía: captured by Texans Oct 1835. Site of the Goliad Massacre on March 27, 1836, where 342 Texan POWs were executed by order of Santa Anna.

label: Battle of San Jacinto
sub: Decisive Texan victory on April 21, 1836. Sam Houston's 910-man force routed Santa Anna's army in 18 minutes. Santa Anna captured the next day; Texas independence secured.

label: Washington-on-the-Brazos
sub: Site where Texas Declaration of Independence was signed on March 2, 1836, and the Constitution of the Republic of Texas was drafted.

label: Velasco — Treaty Site
sub: Location where the Treaties of Velasco were signed by captured Santa Anna on May 14, 1836, ending the Texas Revolution and (disputed) establishing the Rio Grande as the Texas border.

label: Battle of Palo Alto
sub: First major battle of the Mexican-American War, May 8, 1846. Taylor's superior 'flying artillery' devastated Arista's larger Mexican army on open prairie north of the Rio Grande.

label: Battle of Resaca de la Palma
sub: Second battle of the war, May 9, 1846. Taylor's army routed Arista's retreating Mexicans in dense chaparral; Mexican forces fled across the Rio Grande in disarray.

label: Fort Texas / Fort Brown
sub: US earthwork fort constructed by Taylor's troops in March–April 1846, directly across the Rio Grande from Matamoros. Besieged by Mexican artillery May 3–9, 1846. Later became Brownsville, Texas.

label: Matamoros
sub: Key Mexican garrison city on the south bank of the Rio Grande, occupied by US forces on May 18, 1846, after Arista's army fled. Served as Taylor's primary supply and operations base.

label: Corpus Christi — Army of Occupation
sub: General Taylor assembled his 4,000-man Army of Occupation at Corpus Christi from August 1845 through March 1846 before advancing to the Rio Grande. Served as the war's staging ground.

label: Point Isabel — US Supply Depot
sub: Principal US supply point on the Gulf Coast during northern Mexico operations. Taylor rushed here twice to secure supplies while Fort Texas was under Mexican bombardment.

label: Battle of Monterrey
sub: Three-day battle September 21–24, 1846. Taylor's forces captured the fortified city after bitter urban combat; General Ampudia surrendered under an 8-week armistice that Polk later repudiated.

label: Saltillo — Wool's Occupation
sub: Capital of Coahuila state occupied by General John Wool's 3,000-man 'Army of the Center' in November 1846 without significant resistance. Served as rear base for the Buena Vista campaign.

label: Battle of Buena Vista
sub: February 22–23, 1847. Santa Anna's 20,000-man army nearly defeated Taylor's 4,800 Americans. Jefferson Davis's Mississippi Rifles held the crucial V position; US artillery repelled the Mexican assault.

label: San Luis Potosí — Mexican Army Staging
sub: Santa Anna assembled and trained his 20,000-man army here in late 1846 before his famous winter march north to confront Taylor at Buena Vista. Central hub of Mexican war mobilization.

label: Veracruz — Scott's Amphibious Landing
sub: Site of the largest US amphibious operation to date; 12,000 troops landed March 9, 1847. City besieged and surrendered March 29 after heavy artillery bombardment. Scott's primary supply base for march on Mexico City.

label: Battle of Cerro Gordo
sub: April 17–18, 1847. Winfield Scott flanked Santa Anna's mountain fortifications through a path scouted by Capt. Robert E. Lee. Mexican army routed; 3,000 captured, 43 cannon taken.

label: Jalapa (Xalapa) — Scott's Advance Base
sub: Picturesque mountain city occupied by US forces April 19, 1847, following the rout at Cerro Gordo. Scott used Jalapa to rest and resupply before advancing toward Puebla and Mexico City.

label: Puebla — Scott's 3-Month Halt
sub: Mexico's second-largest city surrendered without battle on May 15, 1847. Scott occupied Puebla for nearly three months awaiting reinforcements and the end of yellow fever season before advancing to Mexico City.

label: Battle of Contreras (Padierna)
sub: August 19–20, 1847. US forces used a night march through lava fields to surprise General Valencia's flank. The battle lasted 17 minutes; Mexican line collapsed, opening Scott's route to Churubusco.

label: Battle of Churubusco
sub: August 20, 1847. Fierce battle at the fortified Ex-convent of Churubusco where Santa Anna's army and the Irish-American San Patricio Battalion made their last major stand before Mexico City.

label: Battle of Molino del Rey
sub: September 8, 1847. US assault on the King's Mill complex west of Chapultepec. Costly victory: 116 killed and 665 wounded (25% of the attacking force) for questionable strategic gain.

label: Battle of Chapultepec
sub: September 12–13, 1847. Mexican Military Academy atop Chapultepec Hill stormed by US forces. Site of the 'Niños Héroes' — six young cadets who died rather than surrender. Decisive before Mexico City fell.

label: Mexico City — US Occupation
sub: General Scott entered the Mexican capital on September 14, 1847. The American flag flew over the National Palace for 9 months. The city's fall effectively ended organized Mexican military resistance.

label: Guadalupe Hidalgo — Treaty Signed
sub: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo was signed at this town (now part of Mexico City) on February 2, 1848, ending the Mexican-American War and ceding ~525,000 sq mi to the United States.

label: Santa Fe — Kearny's Bloodless Conquest
sub: General Kearny's Army of the West marched 900 miles from Fort Leavenworth to capture New Mexico's capital on August 18, 1846, without resistance. Kearny proclaimed US sovereignty and established territorial government.

label: Monterey, California — US Flag Raised
sub: Commodore Sloat raised the US flag and claimed California on July 7, 1846. Monterey was California's colonial capital and headquarters of Mexican Alta California government.

label: Los Angeles — US Occupation
sub: Southern California's main settlement occupied by Stockton's naval forces in August 1846, briefly retaken by Californios, then permanently reoccupied after battles of San Gabriel and La Mesa, January 10, 1847.

label: San Diego — US Naval Base
sub: San Diego Bay secured by US naval forces under Stockton in late July 1846. Served as the relief base that dispatched the rescue party that saved Kearny's battered army after San Pasqual.

label: Battle of San Pasqual
sub: December 6, 1846. Kearny's exhausted dragoons were ambushed by California lancers under Andrés Pico. Worst US defeat in California: 18 killed, 13 wounded; Kearny himself among the wounded.

label: Battle of San Gabriel
sub: January 8, 1847. Stockton and Kearny's combined force defeated Californio lancers at the San Gabriel River crossing. Followed the next day by Battle of La Mesa, completing the conquest of Southern California.

label: Sonoma — Bear Flag Proclamation
sub: American settlers seized Sonoma on June 14, 1846, and proclaimed the 'California Republic' under the Bear Flag. The revolt was superseded within weeks when the US Navy arrived and claimed California.

label: Tampico — Occupied by US Navy
sub: Important Gulf port seized bloodlessly by Commodore Conner's squadron on November 14, 1846. Tampico's harbor became a key staging base for Scott's Veracruz amphibious expedition.

label: Mier — Black Bean Episode
sub: Site where a Texan raiding force was captured in December 1842. During a recapture, Santa Anna ordered the Black Bean lottery: 17 of 176 recaptured prisoners drew black beans and were shot.

label: Battle of Brazito
sub: Christmas Day, December 25, 1846. Doniphan's Missouri Volunteers defeated a Mexican force near the Rio Grande south of modern El Paso, clearing the route to Chihuahua City.

label: Chihuahua City — Doniphan's Conquest
sub: Doniphan's Missouri Volunteers occupied Chihuahua City on March 1, 1847, following their victory at Sacramento. Doniphan's 2,000-mile march from Fort Leavenworth is one of the longest in US Army history.

label: Battle of Sacramento (Chihuahua)
sub: February 28, 1847. Doniphan's 900 men and 300+ wagons defeated 4,000 entrenched Mexicans on the Sacramento River north of Chihuahua City with horse artillery and cavalry charges.

label: El Paso del Norte (Ciudad Juárez)
sub: Key Rio Grande crossing point used by Doniphan's forces in late 1846. The village on the Mexican side (today Ciudad Juárez) became an important supply and crossing point for US operations in the Chihuahua theater.

label: San Felipe de Austin — Texas Capital
sub: Administrative capital of Austin's colony and the Texas provisional government during the revolution. Burned by retreating Texans to deny it to Santa Anna's advancing army during the Runaway Scrape of March 1836.

label: Battle of Concepción
sub: October 28, 1835. Bowie and Fannin's Texans repulsed a Mexican force near Mission Concepción outside San Antonio using terrain cover — an early demonstration of Texan rifle tactics against massed formations.

label: Storming of Bexar (San Antonio)
sub: December 5–10, 1835. Texans under Benjamin Milam (killed in the fighting) stormed house-to-house through San Antonio; General Cos surrendered Dec 10 and withdrew south of the Rio Grande under parole.

label: Nacogdoches — East Texas Gateway
sub: Easternmost major Texan settlement; portal for American volunteers entering the revolution. Sam Houston was based here before taking command of the Texan army and leading the retreat to San Jacinto.

label: Austin — Capital of Republic of Texas
sub: Established as capital of the Republic of Texas in 1839 by President Mirabeau Lamar, replacing Houston. Named for Stephen F. Austin. Briefly threatened by Mexican raids in 1842. Became capital of the US state of Texas in 1845.

label: Laredo — Rio Grande Crossing
sub: Key Rio Grande crossing point used extensively during the Mexican-American War. Divided by the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo: the US bank became Laredo, Texas, while the Mexican settlement remained as Nuevo Laredo.

label: Camargo — Taylor's Staging Base
sub: Taylor established his primary forward supply depot at Camargo on the San Juan River, July 1846. Dubbed the 'Mound of Graves' by troops — disease killed thousands of US soldiers in the fetid summer heat here.

label: Fortress San Carlos de Perote
sub: Formidable inland fortress on the National Highway east of Mexico City. Held Texan POWs from the Mier Expedition 1842–43. Fell to Scott's advancing forces in May 1847 as Mexican garrison withdrew.

label: Orizaba — Mexican Garrison City
sub: Mountain city on the National Highway where Scott's advance was briefly threatened by guerrilla activity. The massive snow-capped peak of Pico de Orizaba loomed over the US Army's march from Veracruz.

label: Querétaro — Mexican Ratification Capital
sub: Mexico's temporary capital during the US occupation of Mexico City. President Peña y Peña's government ratified the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo here on May 30, 1848, ending the war.

label: Tucson — Army of the West Passes Through
sub: Mexican garrison town in the Gila corridor. Kearny's Army of the West passed through Tucson (Presidio San Agustín del Tucsón) in mid-December 1846 during the march to California. Mexican garrison withdrew without a fight.

label: San Francisco (Yerba Buena) — Occupied
sub: US Navy Commander John B. Montgomery raised the American flag at the small settlement of Yerba Buena on July 9, 1846, renaming it San Francisco. Gold Rush of 1849 transformed it into one of America's great cities.

label: Sutter's Fort — Gold Discovery
sub: John Sutter's inland California fort; James Marshall discovered gold at Sutter's Mill on the American River on January 24, 1848 — nine days before the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo was signed. Launched the California Gold Rush.

label: Fort Leavenworth — Army of the West
sub: Kearny's 1,700-man Army of the West assembled and departed Fort Leavenworth in June 1846, marching the Santa Fe Trail to New Mexico and then continuing to California in one of history's great military marches.

label: Linares — Mexican Rear Area
sub: Mexican city used as a rear supply area during the Monterrey campaign. US forces under General Taylor bypassed it during their advance on Monterrey, September 1846.

label: National Palace — US Flag Raised
sub: Scott's Marines and soldiers raised the US flag over Mexico's National Palace (Palacio Nacional) on September 14, 1847. Inspires the Marine Corps Hymn reference to 'the Halls of Montezuma.'

label: San Ángel — San Patricio Executions
sub: Site where 16 members of the San Patricio Battalion were hanged on September 10, 1847. The Irish-American and immigrant Catholic soldiers who had deserted the US Army to fight for Catholic Mexico are honored as heroes in Ireland and Mexico.

label: Dolores Hidalgo — Mexican National Memory
sub: Birthplace of Mexican independence (1810), whose revolutionary legacy shaped Mexico's fierce resistance to US invasion. President Peña y Peña's peace government appealed to this patriotic heritage when defending ratification of the treaty.

label: Coleto Creek — Fannin's Surrender
sub: March 19–20, 1836. Fannin's 300-man force was surrounded in the open prairie by General Urrea's cavalry; without water and taking heavy casualties, Fannin surrendered with promised 'honors of war' that Santa Anna never honored.

label: Battle of Salado Creek — Woll's Repulse (1842)
sub: Texas militia under Mathew Caldwell defeated General Woll's 1,200-man Mexican force on September 18, 1842, forcing Mexico's withdrawal from San Antonio after a 9-day occupation. Demonstrated the Republic of Texas could defend itself but remained vulnerable to raids.

label: Presidio San Antonio de Bexar
sub: The Spanish/Mexican military and administrative complex at San Antonio. General Martín Perfecto de Cos surrendered the presidio and Alamo fortress to Texan forces on December 10, 1835 — giving Texans their first major victory.
